content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Let’s get something out of the way immediately: you didn’t start a business because you dreamed of writing blog posts. You started it because you’re good at the actual thing — fixing HVAC systems, selling candles, doing people’s taxes, whatever it is. Nobody grows up saying “one day, I will craft the perfect meta description.”
And yet here you are, staring at a blank Google Doc titled “Untitled document,” feeling the same dread you felt in 10th grade English class when Mrs. Patterson assigned a five-paragraph essay on The Great Gatsby. Except now the stakes are higher, because this blank page is supposed to bring in customers, not just a passing grade.
I feel you. Writing is hard. It’s the kind of hard where you’d rather reorganize your garage, alphabetize your spice rack, or watch a 45-minute YouTube video on how blenders are made — anything to avoid opening that cursor and making it blink at you accusingly.
So here’s the good news: you don’t have to get good at writing. You just have to get good at pointing an AI tool in the right direction. Below are the tools that’ll do the heavy lifting so you can get back to running your actual business — the one that doesn’t involve Oxford commas.
Why This List Exists
You need consistent content — website copy, emails, blog posts, social captions — but every time you sit down to write it, you either freeze up or produce something that reads like a middle school book report.
Dread. Procrastination. That specific flavor of guilt you feel when your “Content Calendar” spreadsheet has been untouched since March.
AI writing tools that turn “I have no idea what to say” into a finished draft in about the time it takes to microwave a burrito.
Let’s meet the lineup.

1. Jasper — The Overachiever Who Actually Learns Your Voice
If AI writing tools were students, Jasper would be the one who shows up with color-coded notes and somehow also has a part-time job. It’s built specifically for business and marketing content, with a Brand Voice feature that studies your existing writing so its output doesn’t sound like a robot cosplaying as your company.
Best for: Business owners who need a lot of content (blog posts, ads, emails) and want it to actually sound like them, not like a template.
The catch: It’s priced like it went to business school — plans start around $59/user/month for the full toolkit. Worth it if content is a real growth lever for you; overkill if you’re just trying to knock out one blog post a month.
limitation: Jasper is a fantastic first draft machine, but it still needs a human pass. Think of it as your very eager intern — talented, fast, occasionally overconfident.

2. Copy.ai — The Swiss Army Knife for Small Teams
Copy.ai is what happens when someone designs a tool for actual small businesses instead of Fortune 500 marketing departments. Over 90 templates cover everything from social captions to email sequences, and its “Infobase” feature lets you store your company details once so you’re not re-explaining your business to a robot every single time (we’ve all been there).
Best for: Solo owners and small teams who need a lot of different kinds of writing without hiring a full-time copywriter.
limitation: With great template selection comes great decision fatigue. You may spend more time browsing options than actually writing. Pick a lane and go.

3. Koala AI — The SEO Nerd Who Actually Ships
If your real goal is showing up on Google (and let’s be honest, it usually is), Koala is built for exactly that. Pick a keyword, pick an article type, and it produces a long-form, publish-ready post — often north of 3,500 words — complete with internal linking and one-click publishing to WordPress, Shopify, or Ghost.
Best for: Business owners running a blog for SEO purposes who want to go from keyword to published post without ten browser tabs open.
limitation: It’s a specialist, not a generalist. Don’t expect it to write your investor deck. It knows one job and does it well — kind of like that one guy at the barbecue who only grills, but grills beautifully.

4. Grammarly — The Friend Who Reads It Before You Hit Send
You don’t need Grammarly to generate a business plan from scratch, but you absolutely need something checking your work before it goes out into the world with a typo in the headline. Grammarly’s AI layer now does real-time rewriting and tone adjustments on top of its classic grammar checks, and it keeps full ownership of your content — no weird fine print.
Best for: Anyone who writes anything — emails, proposals, social posts — and wants a safety net before hitting publish.
limitation: It polishes; it doesn’t originate. Pair it with one of the generators above rather than expecting it to conjure content from thin air.

5. Notion AI — The Assistant Hiding Inside a Tool You Already Use
If you’re already living in Notion for your project management, its built-in AI is basically free real estate. Paste in a meeting transcript, and it’ll pull out action items. Highlight a rough paragraph, and it’ll turn it into something presentable. It’s less “AI writing tool” and more “AI that happens to write,” which honestly might be the least intimidating way to start.
Best for: Business owners who already use Notion and want AI assistance without adding Yet Another Subscription to the pile.
limitation: It’s genuinely most useful once you’ve already built out your workspace. Show up with zero setup and it feels like a fancy notepad, not a content engine.

6. Rytr — The Budget-Friendly Starter Kit
Not every business needs an enterprise content platform with a five-figure annual contract. Sometimes you just need something free (or nearly free) to help you write a product description without wanting to lie down on the floor. Rytr fills that gap — it’s one of the few genuinely usable free options out there.
Best for: Brand-new businesses, side hustles, or anyone who wants to dip a toe into AI writing before committing real money.
limitation: The output tends to need more editing than the pricier tools. You get what you pay for, but “what you pay for” here is still pretty solid for the price of zero dollars.

So Which One Should You Actually Use?
Here’s my dad-joke-certified rule of thumb: if your budget has more zeroes than your patience, get Jasper or Koala and let them do the heavy lifting. If you’re bootstrapping, start with Rytr or Notion AI and upgrade once the content is actually making you money — not just making you feel productive.
And whatever you choose, remember the golden rule of AI writing tools: they’re a writing partner, not a replacement for you hitting “publish.” Read the draft. Fix the parts that sound like a toaster wrote them. Add the one joke only you would make. That’s the 10% that turns “AI-generated” into “actually yours.”
Because at the end of the day, nobody’s business ever failed from a blank page — they failed from staying on the blank page. So pick a tool, write the thing, and get back to doing what you’re actually good at.
(And if none of this works, there’s always the classic fallback: staring at the cursor until it blinks first.)
